# **Advanced Optimization Strategies for Neural Video Compression: An Exhaustive Analysis of the mask2mask Pipeline**

## **1\. The Empirical Landscape of the Neural Compression Challenge**

The comma.ai video compression challenge represents a highly specialized paradigm within the broader field of deep learning and information theory. The fundamental objective is to compress a single 1200-frame dashcam video—originally operating at a resolution of 1164×874 at 20 frames per second, occupying approximately 37.5 megabytes—into an ultra-compact archive. The evaluation criteria eschew traditional human-perceptual metrics like Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR) or Structural Similarity Index Measure (SSIM). Instead, the challenge relies strictly on the minimization of disagreement across two evaluation neural networks: a semantic segmentation model (SegNet) and a 6-Degree-of-Freedom (6-DOF) camera pose estimation model (PoseNet), penalized by the total byte size of the compressed archive.1

The objective function dictating the evaluation is a carefully weighted composite score mathematically defined as ![][image1]. The first term, ![][image2], evaluates the fidelity of the segmentation mask generated by an smp.Unet architecture featuring an EfficientNet-B2 backbone. This network processes only the odd frames (Frame 1 of each consecutive pair) at a downscaled resolution of 384×512, and the metric calculates the fraction of pixels where the argmax class prediction diverges from the golden baseline. The second term, ![][image3], leverages a FastViT-T12 architecture operating on both frames of the consecutive pair, converted to a YUV6 color space at an aggressively downsampled 192×256 resolution.3 This network outputs a 6-dimensional vector representing camera translation and rotation, with the metric computing the Mean Squared Error (MSE) between the original and compressed pairs.5 The final term, ![][image4], represents the compression ratio relative to the original 37,545,489 bytes.

Because the evaluation focuses exclusively on a single fixed video (videos/0.mkv), the optimization landscape shifts fundamentally from generalizable representation learning to pure overfitting and memorization. The theoretical lower bound of conventional video codecs like H.265 or AV1 hits a hard rate floor of approximately 0.55 when attempting to satisfy the neural evaluators.2 To shatter this floor, the mask2mask architecture was engineered, achieving a substantially lower rate contribution of 0.242 while maintaining competitive semantic and geometric fidelity.

## **2\. The mask2mask Asymmetric Generation Paradigm**

The mask2mask methodology circumvents traditional pixel-domain video compression by encoding the minimal semantic information required by the evaluators and synthesizing the rest via a highly quantized generative neural network. The compressed archive consists of three distinct components: a temporally compressed sequence of segmentation masks encoded as an AV1 video (\~190KB), the architecture bytecode defining the generator (\~7KB), and the 4-bit floating-point (FP4) quantized weights of the generator itself (\~165KB).

At decode time, the architecture employs an AsymmetricPairGenerator totaling 308,401 parameters. The asymmetry is derived from the distinct requirements of the evaluation networks. SegNet requires a fully rendered RGB image for Frame 2, while PoseNet primarily evaluates the relative geometric shift (optical flow) between Frame 2 and Frame 1\. Consequently, the architecture is bifurcated into two specialized subnetworks.

The TinyFrame2Renderer is tasked with rendering Frame 2 directly from the 5-class semantic mask. It utilizes an FP16 QEmbedding layer to map the discrete classes into a continuous latent space, followed by a stem of ConvGNAct (Convolution, GroupNorm, SiLU) layers and a U-Net style encoder-decoder featuring residual blocks. The final output is projected into the RGB space via an FP16 1×1 convolution with a sigmoid activation scaled by 255\.

The TinyMotionFromMasks subnetwork is responsible for synthesizing Frame 1\. Rather than rendering Frame 1 from scratch, which would consume excess capacity and disrupt temporal coherence, this network infers a motion field. It ingests the embedded representations of both Mask 1 and Mask 2, alongside their absolute difference and coordinate grids. The decoder branches into three distinct heads: a flow head utilizing a ![][image5] activation scaled by 12 to predict sub-pixel coordinate offsets, a gating head utilizing a sigmoid activation to manage occlusions, and a residual head utilizing a ![][image5] activation scaled by 20 to inject high-frequency corrections. Frame 1 is subsequently synthesized via the differential warping operation: ![][image6], where ![][image7] is the rendered Frame 2, ![][image8] is the optical flow, ![][image9] is the gate, and ![][image10] is the residual correction.

## **3\. The FP4 Quantization Bottleneck and Optical Flow Sensitivity**

To satisfy the extreme rate constraints, the convolutional body layers of the generator are quantized to a 4-bit floating-point (FP4) representation utilizing a custom non-linear codebook with positive levels ![][image11] plus a sign bit. The quantization is applied per-block with a block size of 32, utilizing FP16 scale factors. While this aggressive quantization enables the network to shrink to \~165KB after Brotli compression, it introduces a catastrophic performance bottleneck specifically isolated within the motion subnetwork.

Empirical evaluation reveals a profound disparity between the resilience of the spatial renderer and the motion network. Prior to quantization, the FP32 network achieves a SegNet disagreement of 0.00280 and a PoseNet MSE of 0.00050. Following FP4 quantization, the SegNet disagreement degrades trivially to 0.00284, demonstrating that the RGB spatial rendering is highly robust to 4-bit weight precision. Conversely, the PoseNet MSE deteriorates by a factor of six, skyrocketing to 0.00297. This massive gap fundamentally answers the first open question regarding the differential impact of quantization on semantic versus geometric synthesis.8

### **3.1 The Mathematical Mechanics of Warping and Error Amplification**

The degradation in ![][image3] upon FP4 quantization is not an arbitrary training failure; it is a direct consequence of the mathematical properties of the grid\_sample operator and the error amplification inherent in predicting bounded flow fields.10 The generation of Frame 1 relies on interpolating sub-pixel values from Frame 2 based on the predicted flow field ![][image8].

Let the original FP32 flow field be ![][image8] and the quantized flow field be ![][image12], where ![][image13] is the induced quantization noise at the output of the flow prediction head. By applying a first-order Taylor series expansion, the synthesized pixel error ![][image14] due to flow quantization at coordinate ![][image15] can be approximated mathematically as:

![][image16]  
This equation reveals the critical architectural vulnerability: the error in the synthesized image is directly proportional to the spatial gradient of the rendered image ![][image7]. Because the mask2mask generator hallucinates highly abstract, high-frequency checkerboard patterns to satisfy the specific filter banks of SegNet and PoseNet, the gradient ![][image17] is artificially massive.12 Consequently, even minute quantization errors in the flow vector ![][image18]—stemming from the coarse FP4 intervals—are massively amplified when sampling from ![][image7], leading to significant structural shifts in the output.9 PoseNet, which relies on the geometric consistency of features across the frame pair to estimate the 6-DOF camera pose, is highly sensitive to these high-frequency structural shifts.3

### **3.2 Activation Scaling and FP4 Resolution Limits**

The flow head applies a ![][image5] activation scaled by a factor of 12, yielding a theoretical maximum flow displacement of ![][image19] pixels. In FP4, the spacing between representable weight states is exceptionally coarse.15 If a convolution kernel requires a precise fractional value to compute a sub-pixel shift (e.g., a flow of 0.75 pixels), the quantization of the weights ![][image20] forces the pre-activation output into discrete intervals.

When multiplied by the aggressive scaling factor of 12, the quantization noise in the final layers translates to macroscopic coordinate shifts. Research into optical flow quantization indicates that sub-pixel accuracy fundamentally requires high-resolution feature representations to perform fine-grained linear combinations of basis filters.10 When weights are restricted to 4 bits, the network loses this capacity.19 Traditional neural video compression pipelines often bypass this by transmitting explicit optical flow extracted from a traditional codec to avoid quantized inference errors entirely.8 However, the strict mask2mask limits prohibit external side-channels. Therefore, the motion network must be coerced into a fundamentally different local minimum—one where the distribution of weights perfectly aligns with the discrete intervals of the FP4 codebook.

| Optical Flow Quantization Variable | Full Precision (FP32) State | 4-Bit (FP4) Quantized State | PoseNet Impact Mechanism |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Weight Representation** | Continuous floating-point | 16 discrete non-linear intervals | Sub-pixel combination loss |
| **Flow Resolution** | Infinite sub-pixel accuracy | Coarse, interval-bound steps | Misalignment of feature geometry |
| **Error Propagation** | Minimal spatial shifting | Amplified by spatial gradients | Severe 6-DOF vector deviation |
| **Temporal Stability** | Coherent inter-frame mapping | High-frequency coordinate jitter | Massive MSE degradation |

## **4\. Advanced Discrete Optimization: Beyond Straight-Through Estimation**

The baseline training methodology utilizes Quantization-Aware Training (QAT) with a Straight-Through Estimator (STE) to simulate the FP4 discretization during the forward pass while permitting gradient flow during the backward pass. While QAT is generally superior to naive Post-Training Quantization (PTQ), jump-starting a network into QAT from an FP32 optimum—especially with a high learning rate warm restart—often traps the optimizer in a suboptimal local minimum.23 The quantization noise acts as a massive stochastic perturbation to the gradient, disrupting the delicate sub-pixel balance required for optical flow estimation.9 To isolate a quantization-robust minimum and match the leader's performance (![][image21] in FP4), advanced discrete optimization frameworks must replace or augment STE.

### **4.1 Coordinate Descent for Quantization (COMQ)**

Instead of relying on stochastic gradient descent to adjust weights in the presence of STE-induced noise, the problem of finding the optimal quantized weights can be framed as a strict discrete optimization problem. Coordinate Descent Quantization (COMQ or CDQuant) has emerged as a state-of-the-art methodology for ultra-low-bit precision, particularly in vision models where exact feature preservation is required.25

COMQ operates by minimizing the layer-wise reconstruction error without backpropagation. Given an FP32 weight matrix ![][image22] and input activations ![][image23], the objective is to find the quantized weight matrix ![][image24] that minimizes the Frobenius norm of the output discrepancy:

$$ \\min\_{W\_q} |

| W\_{fp}X \- W\_q X ||\_F^2 $$

COMQ achieves this by iteratively performing coordinate-wise minimization. It updates one weight parameter (or a localized group of parameters) at a time in a greedy order, while keeping all other variables constant.25 Because the comma.ai challenge is a pure memorization task over a fixed dataset of 600 frame pairs, the activation distribution ![][image23] is entirely deterministic, bounded, and easily cached.

Applying COMQ to the TinyMotionFromMasks network offers distinct advantages over standard QAT. First, it bypasses the inherent gradient mismatch of STE, solving the exact discrete objective.27 Second, by minimizing the activation error strictly layer-by-layer, COMQ directly halts the propagation of quantization noise through the ![][image25] activation, preventing the macroscopic flow shifts that destroy PoseNet accuracy. The leader's ability to maintain PoseNet fidelity strongly implies the use of a discrete, gradient-free optimization algorithm like COMQ to finalize the FP4 assignments.

### **4.2 Block-Level Reconstructive Quantization (BRECQ) vs. Adaptive Rounding**

If gradient-based refinement is structurally preferred over discrete coordinate descent, the architecture must transition from layer-wise analysis to Block Reconstruction (BRECQ).31 While older techniques like AdaRound focus on layer-wise adaptive rounding by learning a continuous variable ![][image26] that is pushed to 0 or 1 via a rectified sigmoid 34, BRECQ extends this theory by optimizing across entire functional blocks.37

In the TinyMotionFromMasks architecture, the downsampling and upsampling paths rely heavily on complex residual connections (ConvGNAct \-\> Conv \-\> GroupNorm \+ skip \+ SiLU) to maintain geometric precision. Quantizing a single convolutional layer in isolation introduces noise that fundamentally alters the distribution of the skip connection. BRECQ optimizes the rounding decisions of all layers within a block simultaneously by minimizing the reconstruction error at the block's final output.23 This is critical for optical flow, as the structural integrity of the flow field relies on the flawless interplay between deep semantic features and high-resolution spatial skip connections.37

### **4.3 Learned Step Size Quantization (LSQ)**

The current pipeline utilizes a rigid FP4 codebook with pre-defined levels (![][image27]) alongside FP16 scale factors. This rigidity forces the weights to adapt to the fixed intervals. Learned Step Size Quantization (LSQ) introduces a fully differentiable scaling parameter for the quantizer itself during the training phase.29

By implementing LSQ during the QAT phase, the network can jointly optimize the continuous weight distribution and the optimal codebook boundary clipping. For the motion network, implementing a learnable scale ![][image28] such that ![][image29] provides the network with the necessary degrees of freedom to suppress outlier flow vectors.43 If a specific convolutional filter requires a tight dynamic range to predict subtle sub-pixel flows, the LSQ parameter will dynamically converge to a small value, effectively compressing the FP4 codebook intervals and providing exponentially higher resolution around the zero-mean.44

## **5\. Mitigating Statistical Shifts via Global Affine Calibration (GAC)**

A highly documented phenomenon in the ultra-low-bit quantization of complex vision architectures is the severe statistical alteration in the distributions of intermediate layer outputs, often exacerbated by the folding of normalization layers into quantized convolutions or the precision mismatch between sequential layers.39 In the mask2mask architecture, GroupNorm parameters are preserved in FP16, but the upstream convolutional weights are quantized to FP4.

This precision asymmetry causes a profound distribution shift. The FP4 convolution yields an activation tensor with a discrete, altered variance compared to the FP32 forward pass. When this shifted tensor is passed into the FP16 GroupNorm, the pre-computed or dynamically calculated normalization statistics (mean and variance) are fundamentally skewed, corrupting the feature representation before it even reaches the activation function.46

The PTQ4VM (Post-Training Quantization for Video Matting) framework identifies this exact vulnerability in temporal generation tasks and proposes Statistically-Driven Global Affine Calibration (GAC).37 GAC introduces a lightweight, post-hoc channel-wise linear correction layer of the form:

![][image30]  
where ![][image31] and ![][image32] are compact, 16-bit shift-and-scale vectors learned through a brief calibration phase immediately following quantization.16 The introduction of GAC effectively compensates for the cumulative statistical distortions without requiring any modification to the core FP4 convolutional parameters.49 Because the comma.ai evaluation permits the inclusion of arbitrary architecture bytecode within the 7KB allowance, embedding a static, pre-trained \[1, C, 1, 1\] FP16 shift/scale tensor after critical ResBlock outputs contributes trivially to the archive rate (mere bytes per layer) while theoretically recovering significant quantization-induced variance collapse.16 The bias correction (![][image32]) specifically acts to re-center the shifted optical flow fields, directly addressing the systemic coordinate drift that punishes the PoseNet evaluation metric.

## **6\. Enforcing Temporal Coherence via Optical Flow Assistance (OFA)**

The mask2mask pipeline currently optimizes the loss function symmetrically over isolated frame pairs, treating each of the 600 pairs as independent instances. The loss directly minimizes the final PoseNet evaluation metric. However, PoseNet inherently scrutinizes the temporal and geometric coherence between consecutive frames.51

In the domain of quantized video matting and temporal interpolation, the Optical Flow Assistance (OFA) methodology operates by simulating dynamic consistency constraints during the calibration and QAT phases.37 While TinyMotionFromMasks already predicts optical flow internally, the QAT phase lacks an explicit temporal prior to stabilize the quantization of this flow, allowing the quantized weights to drift into spatially noisy configurations that technically satisfy the loss locally but break temporal smoothness.

To implement an OFA-inspired temporal regularizer without altering the final decoding architecture, the training loss must be augmented. Let ![][image33] denote the pre-computed optical flow field generated by the fully converged, highly accurate FP32 teacher model. During the QAT or COMQ optimization of the FP4 student model, an auxiliary regularization term ![][image34] can be introduced:

$$ L\_{OFA} \= \\lambda |

| \\Phi\_{FP32} \- \\Phi\_{FP4} ||\_1 $$

This term acts as a rigorous semantic regularizer, explicitly penalizing the FP4 motion network for deviating from the continuous flow trajectories of the teacher model.37 Unlike the standard PoseNet MSE loss, which only observes the final warped RGB output and backpropagates through the noisy grid\_sample operation, the OFA loss provides direct, localized supervision to the flow prediction head.39 This forces the QAT optimizer (or the COMQ coordinate descent) to prioritize the preservation of specific semantic boundaries and geometric relationships that are most fragile under 4-bit precision.37 Once the weights are quantized and frozen, this auxiliary loss is discarded, introducing zero overhead to the inflation pipeline or archive rate.

| Training Strategy Element | Target Metric / Vulnerability | Mechanism of Action | Architecture Modification Required |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **COMQ / CDQuant** | FP4 Sub-pixel Flow Loss | Bypasses backprop, matches discrete codebook states | None (Offline Optimization) |
| **GAC (Affine Calibration)** | GroupNorm Variance Collapse | Injects 16-bit shift/scale to realign statistics | Yes (Negligible bytecode size) |
| **OFA (Flow Assistance)** | Temporal Geometric Jitter | Regularizes FP4 flow against FP32 teacher flow | None (Loss function only) |

## **7\. Optimizing the AV1 Segmentation Mask Encapsulation**

The archive rate is heavily dominated by the AV1-compressed segmentation masks, which occupy approximately 190KB out of the \~362KB total. Any reduction in this payload directly improves the evaluation score via the heavily weighted ![][image35] rate penalty. The current methodology encodes the 5-class semantic predictions as a grayscale video by mapping discrete classes to continuous intensity values via the linear transformation ![][image36].55

### **7.1 Vulnerabilities of Grayscale Mapping in Lossy Codecs**

The linear intensity mapping (yielding values of 0, 64, 128, 191, and 255\) forces a purely categorical, discrete variable into a continuous, spatial-frequency domain fundamentally designed for natural image compression. The AV1 codec, utilizing block-based Discrete Cosine Transforms (DCT) or Asymmetric Discrete Sine Transforms (ADST), inherently suppresses high-frequency coefficients to achieve compression.57

When AV1 attempts to compress sharp, discrete transitions between distant grayscale values (e.g., class 0 adjacent to class 4), the quantization of the transform coefficients induces severe "ringing" or "banding" artifacts near the semantic boundaries.7 Upon inflation, when the decompressed pixels are rounded back to the nearest integer class value, these artifacts manifest as hallucinated, misclassified semantic blobs. Because the TinyFrame2Renderer operates deterministically on these masks, any misclassification in the mask directly propagates into profound structural errors in the generated RGB frames, permanently elevating the minimum achievable ![][image2] floor.

### **7.2 Advanced AV1 Parameterization for Categorical Masks**

To minimize the 0.27% round-trip misclassification rate currently observed at Constant Rate Factor (CRF) 60, while simultaneously pushing for higher compression (e.g., CRF 63 to achieve sub-100KB sizes), several highly specialized codec interventions are mathematically necessary 7:

1. **Strict Monochrome Profile Enforcement:** Standard AV1 encoders implicitly allocate bitrate and computational logic to chroma channels (U, V) even for ostensibly grayscale inputs, unless explicitly configured otherwise. Enforcing a strict 4:0:0 monochrome profile (-pix\_fmt gray) completely disables chroma sub-sampling and inter-channel prediction logic, reallocating the entire available bitrate to the luma channel where the spatial masks exclusively reside.59  
2. **Disabling Psycho-Visual Synthesis and Debanding:** AV1 includes advanced psycho-visual tools like Film Grain Synthesis (FGS) and Constrained Directional Enhancement Filters (CDEF) designed to remove banding in smooth natural skies and add perceptual texture.7 For a categorical mask, these filters are actively detrimental. CDEF will attempt to spatially smooth the sharp semantic boundaries, while FGS will inject stochastic noise into the flat class regions. Explicitly disabling these features (--enable-cdef=0, \--film-grain-denoise=0) preserves the high-frequency step-edges critical for accurate rounding upon decoding.  
3. **Disabling Overlapped Block Motion Compensation (OBMC):** AV1 utilizes OBMC to blend multiple predictors from neighbor blocks to reduce prediction errors in natural motion.65 For semantic masks, blending categorical values creates intermediate grayscale states that map to incorrect classes upon rounding. Disabling OBMC ensures block boundaries remain sharp.  
4. **Optimized Non-Linear Intensity Mapping:** The linear spacing (![][image37]) assumes equal probability and equal transform error for all class transitions. If certain class transitions are highly frequent within the video while others are physically impossible or rare, the intensity gap should be aggressively maximized for the frequent borders to increase resilience against DCT quantization. A non-linear mapping optimized via histogram analysis of the original 1200 frames can mathematically reduce the rounding error floor without increasing the bitrate.66

### **7.3 Reduced Resolution Sub-Sampling and Internal Upscaling**

The generator currently ingests and interpolates a 384x512 mask sequence. Reducing the mask resolution prior to AV1 encoding (e.g., to 192x256 or 256x340) yields a quadratic reduction in pixel volume, directly plummeting the required AV1 bitrate. While bilinear upscaling back to 384x512 via a standard script upon decoding will artificially blur the semantic boundaries, the generator network itself can be explicitly trained to ingest these blurred, lower-resolution masks and apply sub-pixel sharpening internally.13 Since the generator has \~308K parameters, embedding the spatial upscaling logic within the TinyFrame2Renderer stem (via learned transposed convolutions or sub-pixel convolutions) offloads the resolution burden from the AV1 payload to the static network weights, allowing for a substantial and highly favorable shift in the ![][image35] metric.

## **8\. Exploiting the PoseNet YUV6 Evaluation Subsampling**

A critical analytical insight into the evaluation pipeline is the precise nature of the PoseNet evaluation phase. PoseNet does not evaluate the original or generated RGB frames directly. Instead, it analyzes the frame pair strictly after it has been converted to the YUV6 color format at an aggressively downscaled resolution of 192x256.

The rgb\_to\_yuv6 conversion process applies a 2x2 averaging filter to the chroma channels (U and V) to achieve data decimation. This mathematical operation implies that any high-frequency chroma variations within a local 2x2 pixel block are systematically averaged out and destroyed before ever reaching the PoseNet input layers. The generator network can exploit this evaluation blind spot to resolve the tension between the two evaluator networks.

SegNet requires dense, complex spatial patterns to trigger its argmax classification thresholds at 384x512 RGB. PoseNet requires smooth, geometric coherence to estimate optical flow. By applying a custom high-frequency penalty to the luma channel during the QAT phase, the network can be trained to restrict its spatial complexity in the luma space (which heavily dictates PoseNet's geometric perception) while simultaneously burying the necessary high-entropy patterns required for SegNet within the high-frequency chroma space.

Essentially, the generator can learn to synthesize checkerboard-like chroma patterns that successfully satisfy SegNet's convolutional filters but are mathematically averaged out to uniform fields during the 2x2 YUV6 decimation, thereby preventing them from confusing PoseNet's optical flow calculations. This exploitation of the evaluation pipeline requires engineering a custom loss function during training that simulates the exact YUV6 decimation and penalizes luma variance heavily while leaving chroma variance unconstrained.

## **9\. The uint8 Round-Trip Simulation and Spatial Filtering**

The evaluation pipeline includes a mandatory float \-\> round \-\> clamp \-\> float cycle, caused by the requirement to write the generator's output to a .raw file as uint8 integers. The generator's output is generated at 384x512, bilinear upscaled to 1164x874, rounded to uint8, and subsequently downscaled back to 384x512 and 192x256 for evaluation.

The baseline methodology successfully integrates a Straight-Through Estimator (STE) to simulate the uint8 truncation. However, the application of STE solely addresses the integer clamping; it ignores the phase-shift error introduced by the continuous spatial transformations. Bilinear upscaling followed immediately by bilinear downscaling does not result in an identity mapping; it acts as a low-pass spatial filter that alters the phase of high-frequency pixels.

Rather than relying solely on STE to survive the uint8 truncation, the optimization loss function must be modified to heavily penalize high-frequency spatial noise in the raw output that would be distorted by this bilinear round-trip. An auxiliary Total Variation (TV) loss applied to the generator's fake1 and fake2 outputs will mathematically coerce the network to produce locally smooth, contiguous regions. Smooth spatial regions are virtually invariant to the bilinear upscale/downscale cycle, significantly reducing the 1-2 pixel mean error currently introduced by the evaluation pipeline. This inherently improves the structural similarity across the frame pair, providing a cleaner signal for PoseNet to track.

## **10\. Alternative Architectural and Training Paradigms**

Given that the core discrepancy stems fundamentally from the precision bottleneck in the flow-warping mechanism, several strategic architectural and training pivots present high probabilities of success without violating the 7KB bytecode constraint or significantly altering the generator size.

### **10.1 Decoupled Optimization and Mixed Precision Asymmetry**

If empirical testing definitively proves that the motion network's sub-pixel flow prediction is fundamentally incompatible with the 16-level FP4 codebook—even after COMQ and GAC interventions—a mixed-precision architectural approach must be evaluated systematically. While previous baseline experiments attempting to run the entire motion network in INT8 yielded worse scores (likely due to codebook mismatch and unoptimized scaling), a localized, surgical precision elevation is theoretically sound.

Instead of elevating the entire motion pipeline, precision scaling should be applied exclusively where the mathematical bottleneck resides. The convolutional stem and the feature extraction layers of TinyMotionFromMasks process coarse spatial structures and can comfortably remain in FP4. However, the final flow\_head: Conv2d(32 \-\> 2, 1x1) requires exact continuous precision to dictate the warping grid. Elevating the weights of this specific 1x1 convolution—which constitutes a mere 64 parameters—from FP4 to FP16 would increase the archive size by roughly 128 bytes. This is an absolutely negligible penalty to the rate metric. This hybrid precision approach ensures the bulk of the parameters enjoy aggressive FP4 compression while preserving the strict mathematical continuity required for final sub-pixel flow estimation.

### **10.2 Flow-Free Direct Generation**

The core vulnerability—sub-pixel flow quantization sensitivity—is entirely a byproduct of the chosen mathematical synthesis operation: warp(fake2, flow). The alternative is to abandon optical flow rendering entirely for Frame 1\.

A revised architecture would utilize a shared latent encoder for the spatial masks, followed by two separate, lightweight decoder heads. One head renders Frame 2, and the other directly renders Frame 1 from the concatenated mask features. By eliminating the grid\_sample warping operation, the severe spatial-gradient error amplification (![][image38]) is entirely bypassed.9 While predicting RGB directly requires the network to learn geometric translations implicitly within its convolutional weights, CNNs are mathematically highly capable of learning translation equivariance given sufficient capacity.14 Eliminating the explicit flow tensor removes the strict sub-pixel resolution requirement, allowing the network to comfortably settle into an FP4 representation where quantization errors manifest merely as localized, high-frequency color variations rather than macroscopic structural warping that destroys the PoseNet MSE.

### **10.3 Curriculum Learning and Progressive Noise Injection**

The leader's success (PoseNet 0.00081 in FP4) strongly suggests a vastly superior optimization trajectory, rather than a secret architectural trick. Jump-starting QAT with a high learning rate shock causes catastrophic forgetting of the delicate flow structures learned during the optimal FP32 phase. A mathematically rigorous curriculum must be applied to traverse the loss landscape:

1. **Unconstrained FP32 Convergence:** Train the model to absolute convergence (e.g., ![][image39] epochs) utilizing cosine annealing with warm restarts to comprehensively map the global minimum basin.  
2. **Progressive Noise Injection (PNI):** Instead of immediately quantizing to FP4, inject uniform stochastic noise into the FP32 weights, gradually increasing the variance over hundreds of epochs until it matches the theoretical quantization noise floor of the FP4 codebook. This anneals the network, forcing the optimizer to flatten the loss landscape and find a wide minimum robust to parameter perturbation.  
3. **Activation-Based Knowledge Distillation (KD):** Use the converged FP32 model as a static teacher. The loss function for the FP4 student should not merely rely on the SegNet and PoseNet evaluation metrics, but must include a layer-wise MSE match of the hidden intermediate activations (![][image40] vs. ![][image41]). This directly limits the propagation of quantization noise through the deep network layers, enforcing functional equivalence at every stage.  
4. **Coordinate Descent Finalization:** Freeze the network gradient and apply COMQ to perform the final discrete assignment of FP4 values, eliminating the gradient approximation errors inherent in STE.25

## **11\. Conclusion**

The profound performance gap in the current mask2mask pipeline—characterized by a 91% disparity in the PoseNet evaluation metric—is not indicative of a flawed architectural paradigm, but rather a mathematically predictable consequence of applying uniform, low-bitwidth quantization to continuous optical flow fields. The grid\_sample operator inherently acts as a severe gradient-based error amplifier, transforming minute FP4 weight discretizations into massive structural shifts that fundamentally destroy 6-DOF camera pose estimation.

Achieving supremacy in this highly specific rate-distortion objective requires transitioning the optimization strategy from blunt Quantization-Aware Training via Straight-Through Estimators toward precise, discrete optimization frameworks. The integration of Coordinate Descent Quantization (COMQ) or Block-wise Reconstruction (BRECQ) provides the mathematical rigor necessary to seamlessly map continuous flow weights into the rigid 16-level FP4 codebook without inducing catastrophic coordinate drift. Furthermore, the implementation of Statistically-Driven Global Affine Calibration (GAC) provides a virtually zero-cost safeguard against normalization folding errors, successfully recovering the variance lost in the quantized intermediate activations.

Concurrently, the pipeline must optimize the AV1 encapsulation strategy through strict monochrome profile enforcement, the disabling of psycho-visual tuning filters, and mathematically non-linear mask mapping to prevent DCT ringing artifacts. By exploiting the specific blind spots of the PoseNet YUV6 evaluation pipeline—specifically the 2x2 chroma decimation—and enforcing temporal geometric coherence via Optical Flow Assistance (OFA) regularizers during training, the mask2mask pipeline possesses the theoretical and empirical capacity to surpass the leading benchmark. The necessary paradigm shift is to treat quantization not as a post-training nuisance to be mitigated, but as a primary, differentiable axis within the optimization landscape.

6. 7. Guiding WaveMamba with Frequency Maps for Image Debanding This work has been supported by the UKRI MyWorld Strength in Places Programme (SIPF00006/1). \- arXiv, accessed April 13, 2026, [https://arxiv.org/html/2508.11331v1](https://arxiv.org/html/2508.11331v1)
